text: The production of propylene oxide (PO) via the electrolysis of propylene using water-derived intermediates is a promising approach for sustainable production of PO. In contrast to current commercial processes that utilize chlorinated hydrocarbons or peroxides, chlorine-containing gases, and acids, the use of water-derived intermediates in electrolytic propylene oxidation appears to hold a greener prospect. Here, we demonstrate an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst, namely, PdPtO$_x$/C, which affords a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% for PO at the cathode at an applied current density of 50 mA cm$^{-2}$ at ambient conditions. The incorporation of platinum into the crystal lattice of palladium oxide helps to maintain high-valence platinum species, which are believed to be critical for their unique functionality toward improving the catalytic performance. Analysis of the reaction kinetics further confirmed that the catalytic conversion of propylene occurs via an electrophilic attack of OO* species. Overall, the versatility of this new concept allows for the selective transfer of oxygen atoms from water to substrates in the absence of any precursors or catalysts, thus paving the way for sustainable oxygenation processes.
